questions.text: The "Analyst" is interested in:
questions.explanation: An Analyst focuses on the problem domain, which involves identifying application classes and the relationships (associations) between them. Solution-oriented details like specific implementation classes or operation signatures are typically the concern of the Designer.

questions.text: "Definition of the system in terms understood by the developer", is called:
questions.explanation: The problem statement is usually a natural language description for the client. The system specification and problem specification are formal documents resulting from analysis that define the system in terms precise enough for developers.

questions.text: "Abbott technique" is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Used in the requirements analysis phase to map parts of speech to model components.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Used in the requirements elicitation phase to help identify use cases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Used in the requirements analysis phase to help classify objects into boundary, control, and entity objects.

questions.explanation: Abbott's technique is a natural language analysis method where nouns in a requirement description are mapped to potential classes/objects and verbs are mapped to potential operations.

questions.text: Sequence diagram helps in:
questions.explanation: Sequence diagrams model the dynamic behavior of the system by showing object interactions. This process helps in defining operations (messages), refining high-level use cases into detailed logic, discovering missing objects, and establishing clear interfaces.

questions.text: The ability to change the system to deal with new technology or to fix defects is called:
questions.explanation: Maintainability refers to the ease with which a software system can be modified to correct faults, improve performance, or adapt to a changed environment.

questions.text: Inheritance simplifies the model by eliminating redundancy.
questions.explanation: Inheritance allows child classes to inherit properties and behaviors from a parent class, meaning common attributes/methods are defined only once, thus reducing redundancy.

questions.text: The description of the problem addressed by the system that is provided by the client is called:
questions.explanation: Both 'Problem Statement' and 'Statement of Work' (SoW) are terms used for the initial document provided by the client to describe their needs and the project scope.

questions.text: Which of the following falls under the "dependability" requirement?
questions.explanation: Dependability is a composite quality attribute that includes reliability, availability, safety, and security.

questions.text: Requirement specifications of the system are said to be correct if:

choices.label: A
choices.text: it represents the system that the clients needs

choices.label: B
choices.text: it represents the system that the developers intend to build

choices.label: D
choices.text: All aspects of the system are represented in the requirements model

questions.explanation: Correctness is specifically the degree to which the requirements correctly represent the client's needs. Representing 'all aspects' is completeness, not just correctness.

questions.text: To Provide the services of an existing system in a new environment is called:
questions.explanation: Interface engineering involves providing an existing system's functionality in a new environment or to a new type of client without necessarily changing the core implementation of the system.

questions.text: The requirements elicitation phase results in:
questions.explanation: Requirements elicitation focuses on gathering requirements and documenting them in a specification (System/Problem specification). The Analysis model is the result of the Analysis phase, which follows elicitation.

questions.text: In "forward engineering", we can identify classes in an existing system.
questions.explanation: Forward engineering is the traditional process of moving from high-level abstractions to implementation. Identifying classes from an existing system (source code) is called reverse engineering.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT true about scenarios:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A textual description of the usage of a system.

choices.label: B
choices.text: is written from an end user's point of view

questions.explanation: Scenarios are meant to be concrete, informal examples of system use. They are not intended to be abstract or formal; those qualities belong more to use cases or formal specifications.

questions.text: Which of the following statements (is/are) NOT true about package in UML?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Used to organize UML models to increase their readability.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Used to model subsystems in a complex system

choices.label: C
choices.text: Usually is an Application domain concept

questions.explanation: Packages are primarily organizational constructs used by designers to group related elements and manage complexity. While they can correspond to architectural components, they are not typically concepts found directly in the application domain (like 'Customer' or 'Account').

questions.text: Which of the following statements (is/are) true about "modelling"?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Used to represent the system in an abstract way.

questions.explanation: Modeling is the creation of an abstraction of a system to help understand complexity, visualize the design, and focus only on details relevant to a particular perspective.

questions.text: When mapping an object model to a database, an instance of a class is mapped onto a:

choices.label: D
choices.text: Instances are not mapped into database elements

questions.explanation: In object-relational mapping, a class typically maps to a table, and each specific instance (object) of that class maps to a single row in that table.

questions.text: Mapping an object model to code is considered:
questions.explanation: Forward engineering is the process of generating code from a design or model. Reverse engineering is the opposite process.

questions.text: In the class diagram below, the developer of class D is (a/an) ________ of class C and (a/an) ________ of class E.
questions.explanation: In software engineering roles, a developer creating a subclass (D) is an 'extender' of the superclass (E). If class D uses class C, the developer is a 'user' of C.

questions.text: Information hiding improves efficiency.
questions.explanation: Information hiding primarily improves maintainability, portability, and readability. It may slightly decrease runtime efficiency due to abstraction layers or accessor methods.

questions.text: Which of the following is/are considered a design issue:
questions.explanation: During the system design phase, designers must address decomposition, concurrency, hardware mapping, and data management strategies.

questions.text: A system with more functionality is considered more usable.
questions.explanation: Usability refers to how easy and efficient a system is for users to use. More functionality often increases complexity, which can decrease usability.

questions.text: High coherence can be achieved if most of the interaction is across subsystems.
questions.explanation: Cohesion (coherence) refers to how strongly related the internal elements of a subsystem are. High interaction *across* subsystems refers to coupling, and high coupling is generally undesirable.

questions.text: The term "API" is used to describe the subsystem "interface" in the ________ phase.
questions.explanation: During Object Design, interfaces (APIs) for components and classes are defined in detail to prepare for implementation.

questions.text: The subsystem decomposition is mainly based on:
questions.explanation: Initial subsystem decomposition is often driven by the functional model to ensure all required features are accounted for in the structural breakdown.

questions.text: "A pattern for a subsystem decomposition" is called:
questions.explanation: Architectural styles (like Layered, Client-Server, or Peer-to-Peer) represent general patterns for organizing subsystems.

questions.text: Which of the following is/are considered in the analysis object model?
questions.explanation: The Analysis Object Model focuses on identifying key domain objects, their attributes, and basic operations. Details like visibility, types, and formal contracts are usually deferred to the Object Design phase.

questions.text: The icon is used to represent:
questions.explanation: In Unified Modeling Language (UML) analysis diagrams, a circle with a vertical line attached to the left represents a Boundary Object.

questions.text: Design usability implies that the interfaces can also be used in other (future) software systems.
questions.explanation: The ability for components to be used in other systems is called 'Reusability', not 'Usability'.

questions.text: While validating requirements, a specification is considered "consistent" if:

choices.label: A
choices.text: It represents accurately the client needs

choices.label: B
choices.text: All aspects of the system are represented in the requirements model

choices.label: D
choices.text: Each system function can be traced to a corresponding set of functional requirements

questions.explanation: Consistency means that the requirements do not conflict with or contradict each other.

questions.text: Which of the following is/are considered non-functional requirements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The system should be easy to learn

questions.explanation: Non-functional requirements specify quality attributes (usability, constraints, performance, reliability) rather than specific behaviors.

questions.text: The "Analyst" is interested in:
questions.explanation: Analysts focus on the domain/application logic and how entities relate. Solution classes and method signatures are the focus of software designers and developers.

questions.text: "Definition of the system in terms understood by the developer", is called:
questions.explanation: The System Specification is the technical document that translates user requirements into terms that software developers can use for implementation.

questions.text: "Abbott technique":

choices.label: A
choices.text: Used in the requirements analysis phase to map parts of speech to model components

choices.label: B
choices.text: Used in the requirements elicitation phase to help identify use cases

choices.label: C
choices.text: Used in the requirements analysis phase to help classify objects into boundary, control, or entity objects

questions.explanation: Abbott's technique is a natural language analysis where nouns are potential classes and verbs are potential methods or operations.

questions.text: Sequence diagram helps in:
questions.explanation: Sequence diagrams model object interactions, which helps in identifying objects needed for a scenario and refining the logic of use cases.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT an output of the requirements analysis phase?
questions.explanation: The analysis phase focuses on the application domain model. The solution domain object model is developed later during the design phase.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is/are NOT true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Boundary object can create control object

choices.label: B
choices.text: Control object can create boundary object

choices.label: C
choices.text: Boundary object can create entity object

choices.label: D
choices.text: Control object can create entity object

questions.explanation: Under Robustness Analysis (BCE pattern) rules, Boundary objects interact with users and Control objects. They should not directly create or interact with Entity objects; that is the responsibility of Control objects.

questions.text: We usually draw state diagram for the object with long life span. This is true for ________:
questions.explanation: Entity objects persist over long periods and have complex states that warrant state diagrams. Boundary and Control objects are often transient (existing only for a specific interaction or task).

questions.text: The relationship between a "House" and a "Room" can be accurately described as:
questions.explanation: Composition is a strong 'part-of' relationship where the parts (rooms) cannot logically exist without the whole (house).

questions.text: Which of the following diagrams helps in requirements elicitation?
questions.explanation: Usecase diagrams are primary tools during elicitation to identify system actors and their required interactions with the system.

questions.text: The description of the problem addressed by the system that is developed by the client is:
questions.explanation: The Problem Statement is typically the initial document provided by the client describing the business need or problem to be solved.

questions.text: Which of the following falls under the "dependability" requirement of a system?
questions.explanation: Dependability is a composite property including reliability, availability, safety, and security.

questions.text: How can domain classes be identified in a UML project?

choices.label: B
choices.text: By identifying noun phrases in the system description.

choices.label: C
choices.text: By using a conceptual class category list.

questions.explanation: Identifying domain (conceptual) classes involves several standard techniques: identifying nouns/noun phrases from requirements text, using pre-defined category lists (like physical objects, transactions, roles, etc.), and reusing established models from similar domains.

questions.text: Does the software engineer add operation contracts in the requirement analysis phase?
questions.explanation: In Object-Oriented Analysis, operation contracts are created to describe the behavior of system operations in terms of state changes to the domain objects, occurring during the analysis phase following the creation of use cases.

questions.text: Frameworks usually contain implementations of many design patterns.
questions.explanation: Software frameworks are built to provide reusable abstractions and generic functionality, which is achieved by implementing various design patterns (e.g., Template Method for hooks, Observer for event handling) that developers can then extend.

questions.text: In application domain objects (domain model), the attribute types are typically shown in the class diagram.
questions.explanation: In a conceptual domain model, attribute types (like int, String) and methods are usually omitted. The goal is to represent real-world concepts and their relationships rather than software implementation details, which are reserved for the Design Class Diagram.

questions.text: Greenfield engineering is defined as:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Developing the software from scratch without constraints of legacy systems.

questions.explanation: Greenfield engineering refers to a project that starts on 'green fields'—meaning there is no previous work or legacy infrastructure that the new system must be compatible with, allowing it to be built from scratch.

questions.text: The use of 'having' verbs (e.g., 'has') in a requirement description helps the requirement engineer to identify which of the following?
questions.explanation: A 'has-a' relationship in natural language identifies that one concept contains or is composed of another. In UML, this translates to either an 'Attribute' (e.g., a person has a name) or an 'Aggregation/Composition' relationship (e.g., a car has wheels).

questions.text:
Based on the provided use case diagram for the **Online Course Registration System (OCRS)**, what is the post-condition for the use case "Add Course"?

```mermaid
graph LR
    Student((Student))
    subgraph "Online Course Registration System (OCRS)"
        RC([Registration Closed])
        AC([Add Course])
        UC3([UseCase3])
        UC1([UseCase1])
        Login([Login])
        UC2([UseCase2])

        Student --- AC
        Student --- UC1

        AC -. "<<include>>" .-> UC3
        AC -. "<<include>>" .-> Login

        RC --- AC
        UC1 --- Login
        Login --- UC2
    end
```

choices.label: B
choices.text: Notification email shall be sends to the student

questions.explanation: A post-condition defines the state of the system after a use case has successfully completed. In a transaction system like adding a course, the final system action is typically sending a confirmation message to the user.

questions.text: What is the relationship between the use case "UseCase1" and the use case "Login"?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relationship "include" and the base case is "UseCase1" and the included use case is "Login"

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relationship "include" and the base case is "Login" and the included use case is "UseCase1"

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relationship "extend" and the base case is "UseCase1" and the extending use case is "Login"

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relationship "extend" and the base case is "Login" and the extending use case is "UseCase1"

questions.explanation: In typical system modeling, mandatory requirements like authentication (Login) are 'included' by functional use cases. Therefore, UseCase1 serves as the base case that includes the Login functionality.

questions.text: The relationship shown in the diagram between the use case "Add Course" and the use case "Login" is correct.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Incorrect, it should be an extend relationship

choices.label: D
choices.text: Incorrect, Login should be the base case

questions.explanation: The diagram explicitly shows a dashed arrow labeled `<<include>>` from "Add Course" to "Login", which correctly indicates that adding a course requires the login process as part of its execution.

questions.text: How many included use case(s) for the use case "Add Course" are shown in the diagram?

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no included use case(s)

questions.explanation: The diagram shows two dashed lines labeled `<<include>>` originating from "Add Course" and pointing towards "UseCase3" and "Login".

questions.text: What is the entry Condition (pre-condition) for the use case "Add Course"?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Notification email shall be sent to the student.

questions.explanation: An entry condition is a state that must be true before the use case can begin. A student must be 'active' in the system as a prerequisite for adding courses.

questions.text: What is the relationship between use case "Add Course" and use case "Registration Closed" and which one is the base use case?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relationship is «extend» and the base use case is 'Add Course' and the extending use case is 'Registration Closed'.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relationship is «extend» and the base use case is 'Registration Closed' and the extend 'Add Course'.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relationship is <<include>> and the base use case is 'Add Course' and the included 'Registration Closed'.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relationship is «include» and the base use case is 'Registration Closed' and the extend 'Add Course'.

questions.explanation: The 'extend' relationship is used to handle conditional or exceptional behavior. 'Registration Closed' is a condition that extends the standard 'Add Course' behavior. Therefore, 'Add Course' is the base use case.

questions.text: What is the relationship between use case "Login" and "UseCase2"?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Relationship "include" and the base case is "UseCase2" and the included use case is "Login"

choices.label: B
choices.text: Relationship "include" and the base case is "Login" and the included use case is "UseCase2"

choices.label: C
choices.text: Relationship "extend" and the base case is "UseCase2" and the extending use case is "Login"

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relationship "extend" and the base case is "Login" and the extending use case is "UseCase2"

questions.explanation: In most system architectures, specialized functions like UseCase2 include a core Login component for security. UseCase2 acts as the base case that includes Login.

questions.text: What is the last step of the basic flow of the use case "Add Course"?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Student clicks ok button on confirmation message.

choices.label: G
choices.text: Notification email shall be sent to the student.

questions.explanation: The final step of a basic flow (happy path) usually involves the system providing the final result or notification to the user. Sending a notification email confirms the completion of the 'Add Course' process.
